data.title: Diamondback Energy: Fast-Growing Dividend Now Has A Tempting Yield
data.description:
The common stock of Diamondback Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FANG) is currently being treated like all the other common stocks as Mr. Market is in the “throw them all out” mood. But eventually things will settle down and quality will matter. Diamondback Energy is usually known for fast growth. The debt rating is investment grade as well. That fast growth includes the dividend, which is now on the high end of the historical yield.

Normally, one does not see a yield like the above for a company that grows as fast as Diamondback Energy. This management “goes the extra mile” by having a very long history of acquisitions that have grown the company (and the stock price) while meeting the market demand for shareholder returns.

Dividends (the variable part) can vary because this company will switch between stock repurchases and a variable payment as market conditions dictate. However, as the yield shows above, things are getting into irrationally cheap territory with a dividend yield that one would expect from a slower growth company.

The latest base dividend is shown above. Growth in per share metrics not only happens from that acquisition program that has proven successful, but also from the share repurchase program that generally adds another percent or so to that growth. The complete program has enabled a growth of both the base dividend and the variable dividend at rates far above the industry average.

Now, as the company gets large, the dividend growth rate of really any kind will slow. However, as long as management continues the acquisition history, this idea could prove to be a solid variable income idea for a retirement portfolio. The company is investment grade, and the stock price rarely gets so low that the overall cash yield is that high.

Last Article

The last article noted that there was yet another acquisition to add to a long line of successful acquisitions. It also mentioned that the CEO would be stepping down. This may usher in a year or two of uncertainty. But it would appear that there has been some succession planning. That should smooth the transition to the new CEO.

Management did announce that the acquisition mentioned in the last article closed on April 1.

The exceptionally low costs are shown by the breakeven price needed to defend the base dividend.

The company has expanded into the Midland Basin because acreage prices are much lower than the Delaware Basin and profitability appears to be every bit as good. Acquisitions can be made at prices that benefit per share metrics. Then, if Mr. Market decides to ignore the stock, then management will buy back shares to make the deal even better. The investment grade rating is maintained by selling any acreage that no longer can compete for capital.

Fourth Quarter

The net result of all of this is that per share metrics grow significantly, as shown below year-in and longer term.

Like any other upstream company, Diamondback is exposed to the commodity price fluctuations. But if management is correct about the benefits of the latest acquisition, the stock price is very likely to recover and move to higher ground during the next cyclical recovery.

The investment grade rating means that this company is in a position to deal with whatever happens in the future. The debt rating here is higher than is the case for many companies I follow. The company has an exceptionally low breakeven point to pay the base dividend as well. This is a very unusual picture in total when one considers this is an upstream company.

With many companies, I follow that base dividend often proves to be indefensible during cyclical downturns because the WTI price needed to pay it is too high. Here, things are considerably better. More importantly, if the dividend needs to be cut, investors can be sure that investment grade rating means it will be back sooner than many other companies I follow.

This management already shows a flexibility that one seldom sees. The acquisition program has slowed the growth of costs in the long term. If debt needs to come down, then assets are sold to repay that debt. There is a firm believe that (if it comes to it) the first loss is the best loss, and then management moves on. That puts this management far ahead of others that often management to magnify a problem or challenge into a fatal situation.

Summary

The overall economic situation has changed enough that the original fiscal year 2025 guidance may well be modified as the fiscal year continues.

This company has low enough costs that it should not be affected as much by the current turmoil as is the case with many other upstream competitors. However, this company can also acquire another company to make up for any production loss should it decide to allow production to decline due to low commodity prices. That gives this management unusual flexibility during a time of weak commodity prices.

There is also the choice to grow the company production at commodity prices that are not profitable for many other companies.

Overall, the flexibility demonstrated by the management strategy reinforces the strong buy idea. This is a company that can be owned during a time of uncertainty like the present.

Good management tends to outperform when it is least expected. While others are waiting for “the storm to pass” (and this is quite a storm), this management is likely figuring out how to improve results that will show during the inevitable recovery.

The oil and gas industry tends to outperform during recoveries. This company will likely be a stock price leader during any recovery. Even if the investor tends to hold through the current period of uncertainty, this is a stock that should treat an investor well during the long run.

Risks

The article is written with the idea that the current situation with tariffs and trade wars is a bargaining type of situation that will be replaced with something that is constructive and permanent. There is no assurance that will happen.

Any upstream company is subject to the low visibility and volatility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn will materially change the outlook of the company.

This company has unusually low costs and an investment grade rating on its debt. It should perform better than many upstream operators during a cyclical downturn. However, there are no guarantees on that idea.

A loss of key personnel could prove to be a major setback for the future of the company.

data.title: Amcor: Dividend Aristocrat Executing A Major Acquisition
data.description:
Shares of packaging concern Amcor plc (NYSE:AMCR) have been range-bound since the November 2024 announcement of its intention to acquire Berry Global (BERY) in an all-stock transaction. Due to close in mid-2025, the combination is expected to wring out $650 million in annual synergies by year 3 plus a one-time $280 million cash benefit from working capital efficiencies. With membership in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats (current yield: 5.7%), the positive reaction from Street analysts concerning the Berry deal and recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview

Amcor plc is a Jersey domiciled, Zurich, Switzerland headquartered global manufacturer of packaging solutions primarily to the food, beverage, and healthcare verticals. The company’s operations currently include 212 facilities in 40 countries, employing more than 41,000. Those numbers will rise considerably upon the closing of an impending merger with rival Berry Global Group (BERY). Amcor was founded as a paper mill in Australia in 1868, changed to its current moniker in 1986, and embarked on series of mergers and acquisitions in the new millennium, the two most significant of which were its $2.4 billion acquisition of assets from Rio Tinto’s (RIO) Alcan unit in 2010 and its $5.2 billion merger with Bemis in 2019, the latter of which provided it with an NYSE listing. The stock trades around nine bucks a share, translating to an approximate market cap of $13 billion.

The company operates on a fiscal year (FY) ending June 30th. For the avoidance of doubt, the period ending June 30, 2025 is FY 2025.

Operating Segments

Currently, Amcor views its performance through two operating segments: Flexibles and Rigid Packaging.

Flexibles consists of the manufacturing of flexible and film packaging for the food and beverage, medical and pharmaceutical, fresh produce, snack food, and personal care products (amongst others). It employs ~35,000 at 160 manufacturing and support facilities in 36 countries, generating FY 2024 Adj. EBIT of $1.40 billion on revenue of $10.33 billion, representing 76% of the company’s net sales. These figures were down 2% and 7% from FY 2023 (respectively) as the unit realized negative impacts from the pass-through of lower raw material costs and sales volumes down 4%, slightly offset by favorable currency translations.

As the name suggests, Rigid Packaging provides rigid containers predominantly for food and beverage products and personal care items, as well as plastic caps for a plethora of applications. It employs ~5,000 at 52 manufacturing and support sites in 11 countries, accounting for FY 2024 Adj. EBIT of $259 million on net sales of $3.31 billion, representing declines of 2% and 7% from FY 2023. Like the percentage declines, the story was somewhat the same as with the Flexibles segment, although the drop in sales volumes was more pronounced at 8%.

Adding in corporate overhead, Amcor produced FY 2024 earnings of $0.70 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $1.56 billion on net sales of $13.64 billion versus $0.73 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $1.61 billion on net sales of $14.69 billion, representing declines of 4%, 3%, and 7%, respectively. That performance was below the company’s ten year (FY 2014-FY 2024) averages that reflected growth rates of 7%, 5%, and 1% (on a comparable constant currency basis).respectively.

Berry Global Acquisition

In a typical year, Amcor would dedicate approximately one-third of its operating cash flow to capital expenditures, about half to dividends, and the balance to acquisitions or share repurchases. That model will be modified somewhat based on its proposed acquisition of Berry Global, a maker of differentiated yet complementary – as far as Amcor is concerned – packaging products in an all-stock transaction. Announced in November 2024, stakeholders in the acquiree will receive 7.25 shares of AMCR for every one share of BERY. In addition to geography, one example of the differentiated yet complementary businesses provided by management was a yogurt cup featuring Amcor’s rigid packaging with Berry’s foil top affixed to it. Additionally, the target had just spun off its health, hygiene and specialties global nonwovens and film business that was then merged with Glatfelter to create the world’s largest nonwovens concern dubbed Magnera (MAGN) two and half weeks prior to the Amcor pronouncement.

Based on TTM (September 30, 2024) metrics and accounting for its recent spin off, Berry is contributing EBIT of $1.7 billion on net sales of $10.1 million, bringing the combination total to EBIT of $3.7 billion on net sales of $23.6 billion before giving effect to anticipated synergies. As for those synergies, management is forecasting the merger to produce $650 million per annum of cost and procurement pricing savings by the end of year three with a one-time $280 million cash benefit from working capital efficiencies that will offset costs to achieve said synergies. As a result, the deal is expected to deliver Adj. cash EPS accretion of more than 35% versus Amcor’s TTM as of September 30, 2024.

Amcor’s acquisition of Berry is anticipated to close in mid-2025.

Share Price Performance

Given the all-stock nature of the deal, risk arbitrageurs have kept a lid on Amcor’s stock price, shorting close to 100 million shares – a position that could rise as the deal closing nears. As such, shares of AMCR have traded in a range of $9.29 to $10.71 since the proposed merger’s announcement. That said, Amcor’s stock has been relatively range-bound since shortly after the pandemic selloff, trading between $8.45 and $13.61 a share since May 2020, with the preponderance of that time spent between $9 and $12 a share.

Q2 FY 2025 Financials and FY 2025 Outlook

Part of the reason for the range-bound nature of its stock price is its low growth profile and the relative predictability of the company’s earnings, with 88% of its net sales derived from non-cyclical verticals such as food, beverage, healthcare, and personal care. Management has only had to modify its annual earnings and Adj. free cash flow outlooks twice in the last half decade: a better FY 2021 emerging from the pandemic; and a worse FY 2023 with inflation impacting margins and demand. Before it announced that it was tying the knot with Berry, Amcor guided the Street to FY 2025 non-GAAP EPS of $0.74 and Adj. free cash flow of $950 million, based on range midpoints.

With the merger not expected to consummate until mid-2025, those numbers were not altered when the company reported Q2 FY 2025 financials on February 4, 2025, posting earnings of $0.161 per share (non-GAAP) and Adj. EBIT of $363 million on net sales of $3.24 billion, versus $0.157 and Adj. EBIT of $352 million on net sales of $3.25 billion in Q2 FY 2024, representing (on a comparable constant currency basis) year-over-year improvements of 5% at the bottom of an Adj. EBIT lines (up 1% at the top line) as margins improved.

With an in-line bottom line versus Street expectations buttressed by positive comments about growing visibility regarding synergies in the Berry acquisition, shares of AMCR rallied 5% in the subsequent trading session to $10.05.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary

Given its proclivity to employ stock as currency in major acquisitions, Amcor’s balance sheet is not overleveraged, reflecting investment-grade net debt of $6.11 billion as of December 31, 2024 for net leverage of 3.3. With its forecasted cash flows in H2 FY 2025, that metric is expected to drop to 3.0 (or below) by June 30, 2025. Assuming the onboarding of Berry, the company anticipates deploying its ~$3 billion plus of annual operating cash flow somewhat equally amongst capital expenditures, dividends, and acquisitions/share repurchases.

Amcor’s dividend is of note, since it is an S&P 500 dividend aristocrat, meaning that it has raised its dividend every year for at least 25 years, dating back to its listing on the Australian Securities Exchange. Its current quarterly disbursement of $0.1275 equates to a yield of 5.7%

Since the announcement of the Berry deal, Street analysts have warmed to the otherwise stagnate company, currently featuring five buy and outperform ratings against three holds and a mean price objective of $11.50 as compared to only one buy against seven holds and one underperform with a mean price objective of $10.50 in the month prior to the Berry merger announcement. On average – and not giving effect to Berry – they expect Amcor to earn $0.74 a share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $13.8 billion in FY 2025, followed by $0.79 a share (non-GAAP) on net sales of $14.04 billion in FY 2026.

Insiders at the company are in agreement with the Street’s change of heart, with CEO Peter Konieczny purchasing 100,000 shares at an average price of $10.05 and board member Arun Nayar buying 100,000 shares at $9.95, both after its Q2 FY 2025 financial report.

Verdict

With its approaching combination expected to deliver solid cash EPS accretion, Amcor would make a solid covered call candidate, as it will likely be range-bound until the consummation of the Berry transaction, has a solid shot of rising after the risk arbitrageur pressure disappears post-close, and pays a solid and safe 5.7% dividend. The problem, unfortunately, is there is little liquidity in the options against the equity. This makes a covered call strategy non-viable. If it wasn't for current global trade uncertainty, I would take a small equity position in AMCR. However, given how uncertain and evolving tariff policies currently are, I am passing on any investment recommendation around Amcor.

data.title: Cummins: Tariff Turbulence Creates A Buying Opportunity (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

I’m a big fan of Cummins Inc (NYSE:CMI). I first recommended the stock in September last year as a ‘buy’ noting that the company was probably among the most underappreciated ‘compounders’ on the market as a steady business that’s been resilient throughout most business cycles. While it’s end markets have some cyclicality, Cummins was and still is poised to benefit from emissions regulations and increasing demand for electrification and alternative energy solutions. However, the stock quickly shot up 23% in December, prompting me to downgrade the stock to a ‘hold’, advising prospective investors to wait for a better opportunity before taking a position.

That seems to have been the correct call (at least so far) as shares have tumbled 28% since then given tariffs and the broader macro situation. While some of that’s attributable to the S&P 500 falling 16% over the same period, Cummins decline has been far steeper and rapid. In this article, I’ll explain why I think it’s time to get back into the stock and take a long position.

Recent Results

While I think most of Cummins’ latest decline can be attributed to the current geopolitical climate and recession fears, I think it’s helpful to put this into context with how the business has been performing up to this point. In the company’s latest quarterly results, Cummins wrapped up 2024 with $8.4 billion of revenues in the quarter, which was a slight 1% decline from last year. Most of this was due to lower North America heavy-duty and pickup truck volumes alongside the impact of the Atmus Filtration Technologies separation. That said, these headwinds were largely offset by strong demand in global power generation markets and a thriving aftermarket business coupled with improved pricing.

For the full year, sales hit a record $34.1 billion which was essentially flat compared to 2023 despite challenges in the second half from softening North America truck demand and the Atmus divestiture. On the bottom line, EBITDA soared despite flattish growth with $6.3 billion in EBITDA – good for a 18.6% margin mostly thanks to the Power Systems segment.

When we look at Q4’24 in particular, I think the story continues to be on margins and improved profitability. As I pointed out in December, Cummins was poised to benefit from favorable pricing and operational improvements and they continued to execute on this in Q4. For example, margins showed notable improvement with EBITDA reaching $1.0 billion or 12.1% of sales, a significant turnaround from the $878 million loss reported a year ago which was heavily impacted by a $2 billion regulatory settlement. Even adjusting for charges tied to the Accelera reorganization EBITDA was $1.3 billion with the EBITDA margin of 15.8% 140bps higher than last year.

Speaking of Accelera, the reorganization of the the business with $312 million in charges reflects a proactive effort by management to streamline operations and position this zero-emission segment for long-term success amid slower adoption rates. To me, I think this was critical as the X15N natural gas engine is now in full production at the Jamestown Engine Plant and gained traction with major North American fleets seeking lower emissions.

Regarding the company’s capital allocation approach, Cummins returned nearly $1 billion to shareholders through dividends while prioritizing debt reduction over share repurchases. The Atmus separation reduced shares outstanding by 5.6 million (enhancing per share metrics without direct buybacks). On capex, the company spent $1.2 billion however most of this went towards supporting investments in the HELM engine platform and capacity expansions like the $200 million commitment to power generation facilities across the U.S., UK, and India. Given that these are mostly growth capex investments as opposed to maintenance capex, I don’t think that detracts from the company’s true cash flow.

In just Q4 alone, the company generated $1.4 billion in operating cash flow and the balance sheet remains healthy with debt to capital of 38.4% and net debt to capital of 26.1%. With total debt of $4.8 billion against EBITDA of $6.3 billion, leverage is around 0.8x which is on the low side for companies in the space. Given long-term issuer debt is investment grade rated (A by S&P and A2 by Moody’s, both with stable outlook), the company can access debt capital markets with a low cost of capital. Also, Cummins’ maturities are long-term in nature and laddered, minimizing refinancing risk.

Outlook

While the recent tariff talks and recession fears might change the outlook, Cummins' management team put together a forecast for 2025 that seems cautiously optimistic. Guidance provided on the earnings conference call post quarter was for full year revenues to range from down 2% to up 3% compared to 2024 which feels like a pragmatic stance given the mixed signals across their key markets. Management guided EBITDA to land between 16.2% and 17.2% of sales, an improvement over the 15.7% figure from 2024. To me, this suggests confidence in squeezing more profitability out of a potentially flat topline driven by operational efficiencies and pricing power. The guidance reflects a tale of two halves with weaker demand expected in North America on-highway truck markets during the first half, offset by resilience in power generation and aftermarket strength throughout the year. So I see this as a balanced view acknowledging near-term softness.

In terms of the segment-level outlook, management is forecasting the Engine business to see revenues between down 2% and up 3% with EBITDA margins ticking up to 14.2% to 15.2%. I think this hints at a short-term dip from North American truck weakness but I believe the long-term story here hinges on the HELM platform’s adoption especially the natural gas variant which could gain traction as fuel economics and regulations shift. Power Systems on the other hand is expected to grow revenues by 2% to 7% with margins climbing to 19% to 20% fueled by sustained data center demand. In my view, this segment’s momentum could stretch well beyond 2025 if infrastructure investments keep pace with tech-driven power needs.

For Accelera, the revenue guidance is for $400 million to $450 million with losses narrowing to $385 million to $415 million. This shows a measured approach to zero-emission investments which I think is wise given the uneven adoption curve. Given headwinds to the Accelera business, management believes it is not currently on track to reach breakeven by 2027, its longtime goal for the segment. In light of a weaker demand outlook and efforts to reduce losses, management continues to pace investments within the segment. The reorganization following a strategic review led to streamlining of the business and a $312M million charge in 4Q24 (of which $305 million was non-cash). Within Accelera, the outlook for battery electric vehicles is favorable for commercial and industrial applications and CMI is well-positioned to capture the market given its battery cell JV (Amplify Cell Technologies), and as such, investments continue in this area. For electrolyzers, investments continue, albeit paced according to slowing customer demand and uncertainty around incentives. Meanwhile, management is being selective in fuel cells and other technologies where adoption continues to push out.

Short-term, I think Q1'25 is likely to be the low point of the year. Cummins' HD truck market build forecast embeds a stronger second half on anticipated economic recovery and pre-buy. Meanwhile, management expects growth in power generation throughout the year. Power Systems growth will primarily be driven by capacity ramp up and continued sales of larger engines in the data center market, along with pricing. It is slightly ahead of schedule in doubling its capacity for large engines by the end of this year. Separately, the parts business is healthy, growing in line with the economy along with some pricing. Distribution continues to do well, and other markets apart from power generation such as mining and oil & gas are expected to recover in the future so I think the tone is overall positive as we look past next quarter.

Longer term, I think there's still a lot of reasons to be optimistic. Through the company's 'Destination Zero' strategy, the company is aligned on an evolving global push towards decarbonization while leveraging their established dominance in diesel engines. The Power Systems segment’s record profitability tied to surging data center demand taps into a secular growth driver fueled by the exponential rise in cloud computing AI and digital infrastructure; trends that are projected to drive global data center power needs up by 160% by 2030. To me, this exposure to a durable long-term demand wave underpinned by tech giants like Amazon (AMZN) and Google (GOOG) needing reliable power generation offers Cummins a clear runway for sustained earnings growth. Beyond that, Cummins' cash generation and strong balance sheet equips them well to navigate any potential macroeconomic headwinds. I'll discuss more of these risks in the next section.

Risks

There are three risks I think are worth pointing out that investors should be aware of when thinking about Cummins' business. The first risk I see is the softening demand in the North America on-highway truck market which management has highlighted as a drag on the first half of 2025. Heavy-duty truck production is projected to range from flat to down 10% and medium-duty down 5% to 15% year over year. If this weakness persists or the expected second-half prebuy tied to EPA27 regulations fails to kick in, revenues and margins in the Engine segment (a cornerstone of earnings) could take a hit.

Another risk would be the slower adoption of zero-emission technologies in the Accelera business. With a $300+ million reorganization charge in Q4 and management conceding that breakeven by 2027 is off track, I worry that prolonged delays in customer uptake or shifting regulatory support could drain cash flow and divert focus from the profitable core business. There's obviously execution risk here that could weigh on performance but this isn't a major driver of revenues and earnings and so the risk here is to the upside if things work out better than expected, in my view.

Lastly, the third risk I’m focused on is the potential impact of tariffs which have resurfaced as a concern with recent developments in U.S. trade policy. In reports from outlets like Reuters and Bloomberg, there have been speculation that the Trump administration is likely to place tariffs as high as 25% on imports from Canada and Mexico. These are key markets for Cummins and as an international company, it's quite clear that business in the short term is going to be impacted. That said, on the earnings call, CFO Mark Smith noted that “to the extent that [they] do incur [tariffs] then [they] think it’s important that the market feels those and [they'll] look to pass those on” suggests confidence in pricing power but I’m skeptical this can fully offset margin erosion if costs spike.

Beyond direct impacts tariffs could ripple through the broader economy potentially triggering inflation or a recession as warned by economists. For Cummins, this could dampen demand for trucks and power generation equipment as customers tighten budgets. In my view, this tariff risk, while not an immediate knockout punch, adds an element of risk that a lot of investors might not be comfortable with. But as I'll discuss in the valuation section, I think this risk is largely baked into the current price.

Valuation and Wrap Up

Cummins is relatively underappreciated by the sellside, even after strong 2024 results. At present, there are currently seven ‘buy’ ratings, 14 ‘hold’ ratings, and two ‘sell’ ratings. From the current price to the average price target one year out this implies about 35.4% upside excluding the dividend. However, I do expect that if sentiment shifts around the economy and businesses pullback spending, it wouldn’t surprise me to see a few cuts to target prices after Q1’25 results.

At the current price through, I think a lot of bad news has already been priced in. For 12.0x P/E and 7.5x EV/EBITDA, I think investors are getting a pretty good deal considering just how expensive Cummins was three months ago (source: Bloomberg). While 2025 and maybe even 2026 might be a bit challenging as the company works through a more challenging macro environment, this seems to be baked into the share price. Also, Cummins trades inexpensively to peers that trade at an average multiple of 10.9x P/E.

Ultimately, while shares have taken a beating, I see value in today's prices which is why I'm shifting my stance back to ‘buy’ from ‘hold.’ The company’s Power Systems strength and alignment with long-term trends like decarbonization and data center growth are long-term positives that I think will outweigh short-term tariff and recession concerns. While there could be some doubt that 2025 numbers aren't met because of near-term disruptions, Cummins has the balance sheet to support future growth and as was put by CEO Jennifer Rumsey, “the slower and messier this goes [their] view is the better it is strategically for Cummins” as one of the leaders in the industry. At 12.0x P/E and 7.5x EV/EBITDA, I see a bargain for a proven compounder with risks already baked in offering a strong entry point for the long haul.

data.title: Solid Biosciences: A Strong Buy As An Emerging Biotech In The Gene Therapy Space
data.description:
What Happened

In our opinion, Solid Biosciences Inc. (NASDAQ:SLDB) may be repositioning itself in a space that has very recently come under considerable scrutiny. Though still in early-stage trials, its lead candidate has shown encouraging results.

Meanwhile, we believe recent events involving Solid's competitor, Sarepta Therapeutics, Inc. (SRPT), may have weighed unfairly on Solid's stock price. Specifically, shares of Sarepta fell around 26% after announcing a fatality in its Elevidys program. In response to this news, shares of Solid dropped around 8%. Subsequently, European regulators halted three clinical trials involving Sarepta's Elevidys.

We initiate a Strong Buy rating on Solid, as we believe that Sarepta should be able to demonstrate that the patient's death was isolated and that Elevidys has a solid safety profile. In turn, Solid may benefit through no direct action of its own, while it continues to advance its pipeline.

Pipeline

SGT-003, Solid’s lead program, is currently in the Phase 1/2 INSPIRE DUCHENNE trial for Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD). Six patients have been dosed as of February 2025. Early biopsy data from the first three participants showed average microdystrophin expression of around 110% by western blot and favorable biomarker shifts. To date, no patients in the study who have received SGT-003 have experienced serious adverse side effects.

Solid is also developing SGT-212 for Friedreich’s ataxia, which received FDA Fast Track designation in early 2025. SGT-212 is expected to undergo Phase 1b dosing in the second half of 2025.

Finally, SGT-501 is under preclinical development for catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (CPVT). The company expects an IND submission for the first half of 2025.

Market Opportunity

The DMD gene therapy market is growing and is expected to increase from $2.3 billion in 2023 to over $5.2 billion by 2033. While Sarepta is currently the only player with an approved treatment for this indication, it is only a matter of time before a competing drug enters the market. We believe Solid is in a strong position to do just that with SGT-003.

In our view, SGT-003 could end up showing real advantages over Sarepta’s Elevidys if what we are seeing in the early data continues to show up in longer-term results.

For one, data from Solid’s ongoing Phase 1/2 trial showed that SGT-003 led to microdystrophin expression around 110% on western blot, though the company has also referenced internal results suggesting it might go over 100% of normal levels. That compares to Sarepta’s Elevidys, which showed a mean micro-dystrophin expression levels ranging from approximately 34% to 51% of normal,

Another thing to watch is that SGT-003 includes the R16 and R17 parts of the dystrophin protein, which help move nitric oxide synthase (nNOS) to where it should be in the muscle. Sarepta’s drug does not include this piece. That matters because nNOS helps protect muscles during physical stress, which is something people with DMD typically lack.

Lastly, Solid’s treatment has been safe so far. No serious problems have been reported in the first few patients. That does not prove it is safer, but it stands in contrast to the tragic death recently reported with Sarepta’s gene therapy, which has naturally raised safety concerns to the extent that European regulators have halted three trials involving the drug.

Sarepta Developments

We previously covered Sarepta and stated that the fatality reported in March 2025 appeared to be isolated. The deceased patient reportedly had undiagnosed cytomegalovirus, which the treating physician believes may have played a role in the outcome. No additional deaths have been reported as of this writing and over 800 patients have received the drug in clinical trials or as a prescribed therapy.

That said, European regulators have taken precautionary action by temporarily halting certain Elevidys trials while further evaluation proceeds. We believe these developments have created a domino effect for peer companies, such as Solid, which relies on a similar AAV-based delivery strategy. In our view, the downside pressure on SLDB has also been exacerbated by broader market turmoil, which has made investors more skittish across early-stage biotech generally. The market has pulled back on Solid stock not because of data generated by Solid, but because of a perceived class-wide risk that, we think, is likely overstated.

If Sarepta can ultimately clarify that the event was unrelated to the gene therapy vector itself — and we believe it will be able to — we believe investor sentiment could stabilize. And if that happens, SLDB may be positioned to recover some of the ground it lost on the news of the patient's death.

Financial Overview

As of December 31, 2024, Solid Biosciences reported $148.9 million in cash, equivalents, and marketable securities. In February 2025, the company raised an additional $200 million via an underwritten public offering. Management expects this combined capital should last into the first half of 2027, which, we assume, is barring major unplanned trial expansions.

Risks

It is worth noting that while we are bullish on Solid Biosciences, considerable risks remain that make any investment in the stock high risk. In particular, if data regarding any of its programs underwhelm, we would expect the stock to plummet.

Also, while we do not expect this to occur, Sarepta could reveal that the patient's death after receiving Elevidys was primarily caused by Elevidys. If this occurs, it could undermine SGT-003's perceived safety profile and cause the stock to falter. Lastly, while we believe Solid has a strong financial position, it is still burning significant amounts of cash. Delays or clinical failures could shorten the cash runway and risk dilutive capital raises.

Conclusion

We believe Solid Biosciences is still early, but on track to disrupt a growing market with limited treatment options. If the safety profile of SGT-003 continues to hold in upcoming updates, and if Sarepta reveals that the patient's death involving Elevidys was an isolated event, sentiment around AAV platforms such as Solid's could rebound.

The gene therapy space has never been for the faint of heart. Still, we think the current pullback in SLDB may be overdone. For these reasons, we rate Solid Biosciences a Buy.

data.title: Charles Schwab: Getting Cheaper, But Not Cheap Enough
data.description:
With a market capitalization of $125.2 billion, The Charles Schwab Corporation (NYSE:SCHW) is a giant in the financial industry. Furthermore, the business has actually done quite well relative to the broader market in recent months. As you know by now, 2025 has not been a good time for long-oriented investors. The trade wars started by the US and the prospect of what could be a global recession have weighed heavily on the sentiments of investors. Since I last reaffirmed Charles Schwab as a "hold" candidate, however, in an article published in January of this year, shares have pulled back by only 3.4%. By comparison, the S&P 500 is down a whopping 12.6%.

Normally, whenever I rate a company a "hold," I am making the claim that shares will likely perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future. So clearly, I was overly pessimistic during this time. However, this doesn't mean that I'm ready to upgrade the company just yet. To be clear, Charles Schwab has made some notable progress in key areas. But there are other areas that are problematic. Shares are getting cheaper compared to where they were a few months ago. But the business is not yet cheap enough in my book to justify a bullish assessment.

Growth has been mixed

Each and every month, the management team at Charles Schwab comes out with some financial data for shareholders. When I last wrote about the firm, investors had access to data covering through November of last year. Today, the results now extend through February of this year. The first of these data points that I think we should look at would be total client assets. The November data showed that the company had $10.31 trillion of client assets.

At the time, this was the highest value of assets in the company's history. Since then, things have been a bit more complicated. Market conditions have worsened, and the business has seen a decline in the value of the assets on its books. Between December and February, net market losses for the company amounted to $162.5 billion.

The end result here was a slight decline to $10.28 trillion. The picture would have been worse had it not been for the fact that, during that three-month window, Charles Schwab experienced an inflow of net new assets. This inflow amounted to $137.3 billion. To put this in perspective, the net new assets added to the company's books in the same three-month window one year earlier came out to only $92 billion. So if we ignore market losses, things are going quite nicely for the enterprise. Naturally, when March data comes out, we should expect significant market losses. And that's because of the plunge that the stock market experienced that month.

There were some other growth areas for the company. As an example, we need only look at the number of active brokerage accounts that the business has. In November, the financial behemoth boasted 36.22 million active brokerage accounts. By February, this number had increased to 36.86 million. During the three months covered, the company saw the addition of 1.23 million brokerage accounts, with the rise in active brokerage accounts totaling 639,000. In fact, other than when the company acquired other players in the space, the 431,000 and 433,000 brokerage accounts added to the company in December of last year in January of this year ended up being the only times in its history that it saw over 400,000 new brokerage accounts added per month. That same two-month window saw the largest gain in active brokerage accounts as well, with 234,000 in December and 253,000 in January.

This is not to say that everything has been great. If I said that, I would be lying to you. For starters, while the company has seen average interest-earning assets increase over time, we have seen a bit of weakness on this front. After rising from $425.79 billion in November of last year to $431.52 billion in January of this year, there was a subsequent drop to $424.81 billion in February. Interest-earning assets are incredibly important since, as the name suggests, the company can generate interest from them while they are on its books.

One area of perpetual weakness for the firm over the last few years now has been its bank account deposits. Bank account deposits are incredibly valuable to financial institutions because they tend to be a source of low-cost capital. But when interest rates rise, investors have a tendency to shift the assets that were previously in their bank accounts into higher-yielding assets. Specific examples here include fixed income investments and money market funds. After all, who wouldn't want a higher yield with little to no risk when it's available? This is why, not only for Charles Schwab, but for many financial firms that accept bank account deposits, there has been considerable weakness over the last couple of years. Banks, for instance, have had to compete for assets by offering higher yields. The end result has been a decline in net interest margin.

At some point, interest rates will drop. However, that is unlikely to be the case this year. Given the tariff situation that we are dealing with, a situation that should be highly inflationary, interest rates will have to remain elevated in order to combat said inflation. Even as bank account deposits have been falling, with a decline from $91.6 billion in February of last year to $83 billion in February of this year, money market funds have exploded. By February, they had hit an all-time high of $625.4 billion. This is up from the $586.1 billion that they were at in November, and it's up from the $505.5 billion on the company's books in February 2024.

One very positive thing that I could point to involves the valuation of the company. When I last wrote about the firm in January, the business was trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 28.2. Between changes in its financial performance and the slight decline in price that shares experienced, the multiple has dropped to 23.4. As the chart below illustrates, this is around the midpoint of where the company has traded for the last five years. The data below also shows the price-to-book multiple. When I wrote about the business earlier, it was trading at a price-to-book multiple of 3.48. Now, that number is down to 3.24. This is certainly cheaper, but honestly, I would like to see both of these drop more. Having a price-to-earnings multiple comfortably below 20 and a price-to-book multiple of 3 or lower would be desirable, as I see it.

Takeaway

Based on the data provided, it appears to me as though Charles Schwab is still doing a fine job. Because of volatile market conditions, the firm has not grown in all key areas that I would have liked. High interest rates continue to negatively affect its bank account deposit situation as well. On top of this, the company is not as cheap as I would prefer it to be.

On the other hand, its number of brokerage accounts is rising nicely and other key aspects of the business like its money market accounts continue to grow. At the end of the day, I still feel most comfortable assigning it a "hold" rating as opposed to a "buy."

data.title: Xero: Outstanding Business, But Trading Above Fair Value
data.description:
The Investment Thesis

Xero (OTCPK:XROLF) (ASX:XERO) is the market leader in cloud accounting software in Australia and New Zealand, and it is expanding into larger addressable markets (North America and the U.K.).

The company operates in a highly profitable industry, providing a recurring product with high retention rates.

After investing for over a decade in building a competitive product, Xero has reached enough scale to be profitable and is increasing its margins at a fast pace.

Company Overview

Xero was founded in 2006 by Rod Drury, who still holds a 5.7% stake in the company, and has rapidly grown to become the market leader in software accounting in the ANZ region, with over 2.4 million users and a market share of around 70%.

The market share gains have been at the expense of MYOD, which had an 80% market share in the region a decade ago.

Xero was one of the pioneers in offering a cloud user-friendly platform for accounting, payroll, and payments, and has aggressively invested to improve its product and expand into new markets.

After its success in New Zealand and Australia, the company expanded into the U.K., the U.S., Canada, South Africa, Singapore, and Denmark, gaining ~1.7 million users over the last decade.

The main focus from a customer perspective is on self-employed individuals, micro and small businesses, and accountants, which collectively represent 90% of Xero's addressable market.

These small businesses primarily demand accounting and payment services, with less demand for payroll services. On average, Xero generates NZ$43 monthly per user.

Its product has translated into an impressive financial performance, with revenues delivering a 43% CAGR over the last decade.

This impressive growth has also been reflected in its share price, widely outperforming the market. Xero initially went public on the New Zealand stock exchange in 2007, raising NZ$15 million, and was listed in Australia in 2012 to fund its international expansion.

Since then, the stock has delivered an impressive 24.6% CAGR.

A Sticky Product

One of the greatest attributes of Xero's business model is the stickiness of its products.

Once a business starts using the product, it has all the data and records on the Xero platform. While it can migrate to a competitor, it will take some time and effort, and there is the risk of losing some of the data.

Considering the reduced price of Xero compared to the overall costs of running a business, small companies look for the most convenient and user-friendly platform, and Xero is one of them.

In addition, when an accountant gets used to the Xero platform, it is unlikely it will change to a competitor, and he will add all its new clients to the platform.

The strong market share in ANZ creates a virtuous cycle for Xero. Since most companies already use the platform, it is required that bookkeepers are experienced with the product.

When looking for accounting jobs in the region, most of the offers require experience in Xero.

From my personal experience, I have been using SAGE when working as a CFO in Europe. It is a great product for bigger companies if you need to manage inventory, production, and accounting, but it is not user-friendly at all.

However, I'd have never shifted to a competitor, since the migration process is a nightmare and not worth the cost saving unless you have serious issues with the software.

The stickiness of accounting software can be stated in Xero's churn rates, which are ~1%.

Margins Expansion

To compete with larger accounting software providers such as Intuit (NYSE:INTU) with its QuickBooks, Xero has been investing a significant portion of its revenues into improving the product and achieving bigger scale.

Over the last decade, Xero has invested over NZ$2.8B in developing its product.

Due to its smaller size, Xero had to invest a higher percentage of revenues. While Intuit spends 17% of its revenues in R&D, Xero is still spending 34% of its revenues on development costs, but I expect this rate to go down looking forward.

Xero's aggressive reinvestment strategy kept operating margins negative until 2019, but they have been steadily growing, and the company is already profitable.

As Xero gets bigger, it benefits from operating leverage and scale efficiencies, but not just from an R&D perspective.

Gross margins have increased from 70% in 2015 to over 88%. Cost of goods include cloud infrastructure, hosting services, and data sourcing, which have decreasing marginal costs.

Selling and administrative expenses have also decreased from 94.6% of revenues a decade ago to 42.3% in FY 2024, given that sales teams don't need to grow as fast as revenues due to Xero's subscription model.

Long-Term Growth Expectations

From a revenue perspective, Xero is still growing above 20% per year. During the first half of FY 2025, it delivered 24% y/y growth in ANZ and 25% y/y in international markets.

Since it already has a significant market share in ANZ, growth in the region is mainly driven by price rises plus a 9% increase in subscribers.

However, Xero's market share is still small in other regions, and the addressable market is much larger, leaving significant room for growth.

The U.S. market is over six times larger compared to the ANZ, and Xero only has a 1% market share.

To increase its market share, Xero is heavily investing in revenue growth in the region, and during H1 2025, 74% of sales and marketing expenses were allocated to international markets.

Also, the company is seeking strategic acquisitions to improve its functionalities, such as the partnership with BILL (NYSE:BILL).

In my view, expanding in the U.S. is the biggest challenge for Xero due to the strong position of QuickBooks, but small market share gains would translate into significant revenue growth.

In the U.K., where the addressable market is double the size compared to Australia, Xero has already captured a 15% market share.

The U.K. market is more fragmented compared to the U.S. market, which is heavily dominated by QuickBooks. During the last five years, the number of Xero subscribers has doubled in the U.K., and the acquisition pace is not slowing down.

Looking at margins, Xero has not yet unlocked its full potential. In my view, over the long term, operating margins could double from the 15.1% delivered in FY 2024.

During the first half of 2025, which tends to be the weaker half of the year, Xero delivered a 17.7% operating margin, compared to 8.7% a year ago.

If we take Intuit as a reference, before acquiring Credit Karma and Mailchimp in 2020 and 2021, the company had operating margins of ~27% and free cash flow margins above 30%.

As Xero becomes larger, its margins should converge to those numbers due to its operating leverage.

FY 2025 Results Forecast

On the May 15, Xero will release its full-year results after a strong first half of the year, delivering 25% y/y growth in revenues and a 76% increase in net income.

For the full year, management was guiding for operating expenses representing 73% of revenues.

However, in my view, Xero could deliver better-than-expected operating margins. During the first half, the company already improved its operating margins compared to the first half of 2024.

In addition, if we look at Intuit's results for the months between November and January, QuickBooks Online delivered a 22% y/y growth, and the desktop platform had 23% y/y growth.

These strong results, taking into account that QuickBooks is a more mature product with a greater market share, lead me to be optimistic about Xero's results for the second half.

The analyst consensus is forecasting NZ$2.1B in revenues and a 27% y/y growth in EPS.

While I agree with the revenue estimates, I forecast better bottom-line results.

During the first half of FY 2025, SG&A costs increased by 9%, and I expect this trend to continue during the second half.

Considering that revenues are growing above 20%, operating margins could increase to 18.5% for the full year, delivering a 35% growth in net income to NZ$236M.

Valuation

Given its dominant position in ANZ, the strong revenue growth, and the increasing margins, the market is assigning a premium to Xero's stock, which is currently trading at 48x the next twelve months' free cash flow (FY 2026).

While I do not doubt the future performance of the company and I expect a significant increase in margins and strong revenue growth due to its small market share in North America, the main question is if the premium is justified.

I am using a 9% discount rate based on a 1.03 beta, a 4% cost of debt, and a 9.15% cost of equity. The share count is expected to increase by 1.5% per year due to stock-based compensation.

Assuming the decline in growth rates presented in the model below as the company becomes bigger, and an increase in operating margins due to operating leverage, with a 4.5% growth rate after 2034 and a 30x LTM FCF multiple, the stock is currently overvalued.

With an average fair value of NZ$125 ($70), Xero's stock is overvalued by 15.6%.

Risks

The main risk for Xero's investment thesis is an unsuccessful expansion into North America.

The company is allocating significant funds to expand the sales and marketing teams in the region, and for now, it only represents 6% of total revenues.

While the company has been successful in gaining market share in the U.K. and ANZ, it is having a tough time competing with QuickBooks.

Despite the significant investments and lower prices compared to QuickBooks, market share gains have been modest.

As stated before, accounting software has low churn rates, and if Xero is not able to translate the investment in the region into revenues, it could affect its valuation, which implies strong growth.

From a short-term perspective, an economic slowdown would lead to fewer business formations and higher bankruptcy rates, lowering demand for Xero's products.

Conclusion

Xero operates in a highly profitable industry with high retention rates. After investing in the development of a competitive product over the last decade, the company has gained significant market share in Australia, New Zealand, and the U.K.

Looking forward, the company will leverage its scale efficiencies to increase operating margins, driving strong bottom line growth.

The desirable qualities of its business model have rewarded Xero's stock with a premium. However, the current price implies high double-digit growth for almost a decade, with a significant expansion in margins.

While the company could justify its current valuation if it successfully gains market share in the U.S., it does not offer enough margin of safety.

As a result, I assign it a hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Sources: S&P LCD, S&P UBS LL.

Performance Review

The Fund (Institutional Class) returned 0.21 percent for the fourth quarter, compared with the 0.17 percent return for its benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index. Year to date, the returns for the Fund and its benchmark were 8.07 percent and 8.19 percent, respectively. Performance over the fourth quarter benefitted from strong credit selection in basic industry as well as strong performance by bank loans, which were less impacted by the rise in interest rates. This was partially offset by softer performance by holdings in the consumer cyclicals sector.

Strategy and Positioning

Market conditions were choppy amid inflation reports, interest rate volatility, and changing expectations for U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts. New issuance volume during the quarter was lower than the first three quarters of the year, in line with seasonal trends. The Fund remained selective in participating in new issues and continued to uncover opportunities in the secondary market.

The Fund had some holdings either called or paid down, with proceeds reinvested in short-duration bonds. The Fund remains positioned up in quality versus the benchmark, driven by an underweight to CCCs. Overall, we remain nimble to take advantage of opportunities in the market using our bottom-up approach.

While spreads remain tight, all-in yields offer closer to average historical value. There continue to be opportunities to invest in credits that are trading cheap to their fundamentals and their risk of default/loss.

Market Review

High yield delivered positive returns for the ninth consecutive quarter in the fourth quarter.

High yield generated mixed performance across most sectors in the quarter. Top performers were technology and communications, which continued to rebound from the negative returns in the first half of the year supported by higher carry. Smaller sectors, including real estate investment trusts (REITs) and utilities, lagged in the fourth quarter.

Lower quality bonds significantly outperformed this quarter, with CCCs returning 2.26 percent, followed by Bs at 0.32 percent, and BBs underperforming at -0.49 percent. Similarly, CCCs returned 15.09 percent, Bs 7.38 percent, and BBs 6.30 percent for the full year.

U.S. high yield new issuance volume totaled $46 billion in the fourth quarter and was up 14 percent compared to the fourth quarter of 2023. For the full year, issuance totaled $282 billion, up 60 percent compared to 2023 and closer to issuance levels exhibited prior to COVID. Refinancing volume continues to drive the primary market, comprising 57 percent of issuance in the fourth quarter versus 63 percent in third quarter. Notably, the market did see an uptick in dividend and mergers and acquisitions (M&A) deals relative to the prior quarters.

High yield experienced net inflows of $2 billion from observable sources split 60/40 between exchanged-traded funds (ETFs) and mutual funds, respectively. Overall, high yield has experienced a total of $15.6 billion of inflows for the year which supported the market.

High yield fundamentals, as measured by net leverage and interest coverage of public issuers, remain in line with their historical averages. The high yield last twelve months default rate of 1.5 percent increased 0.1 percent from last quarter and remains below its long-term average of 4 percent. The distress rate continued to decline to 4 percent from 5.1 percent in September supported by the rally in CCCs.

Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Bps (basis points): One basis point is equal to 0.01%. Duration: A measure of the sensitivity of the price of a bond to a change in interest rates. In general, the higher the duration the more the bond’s price will change with interest rate movements. Spread: Spread refers to the difference between yields on different debt instruments and is used to understand the relative value and risks of different debt instruments.

S&P bond ratings are measured on a scale that ranges from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). Bonds rated BBB- and above are considered investment-grade while bonds rated BB+ and below are considered speculative grade.

Risk Considerations. This fund may not be suitable for all investors.

In general, the value of a fixed-income security falls when interest rates rise and rises when interest rates fall. Longer term bonds are more sensitive to interest rate changes and subject to greater volatility than those with shorter maturities. During periods of declining rates, the interest rates on floating rate securities generally reset downward and their value is unlikely to rise to the same extent as comparable fixed rate securities.

The fund’s exposure to high yield securities may subject the fund to greater volatility.

The fund may invest in derivative instruments, which may be more volatile and less liquid, increasing the risk of loss when compared to traditional securities. Certain of the derivative instruments are also subject to the risks of counterparty default and adverse tax treatment.

The fund’s use of leverage, through borrowings or instruments such as derivatives, may cause the fund to be more volatile than if it had not been leveraged.

Instruments and strategies (such as borrowing transactions and reverse repurchase agreements) may provide leveraged exposure to a particular investment, which will magnify any gains or losses on those investments.

The fund may invest in foreign securities which carry additional risks when compared to U.S. securities, due to the impact of diplomatic, political or economic developments in the country in question (investments in emerging markets securities are generally subject to an even greater level of risks).

Investments in syndicated bank loans generally offer a floating interest rate and involve special types of risks.

The fund’s investments in other investment vehicles subject the fund to those risks and expenses affecting the investment vehicle.

The fund’s investments in restricted securities may involve financial and liquidity risk.

You may have a gain or loss when you sell your shares.

It is important to note that the fund is not guaranteed by the U.S. government.

Please read the prospectus for more detailed information regarding these and other risks.

Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch, and S&P is Bal/BB+/ BB+ or below. The index excludes emerging market debt.

data.title: ONE Gas: Valuation Looks Attractive At First, But This Does Not Tell The Whole Story
data.description:
ONE Gas, Inc. (NYSE:OGS) is a regulated natural gas utility that serves approximately 2.3 million customers in Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas:

This is sufficient to make the company one of the largest natural gas utilities in the United States, with a very similar customer count to that of Southwest Gas Holdings, Inc. (SWX), which we discussed a few days ago. Despite the similar customer count, though, ONE Gas has a much lower market capitalization:

We can see that despite the very similar customer count, ONE Gas is trading at a lower valuation than Southwest Gas Holdings. ONE Gas also has a somewhat lower forward price-to-earnings ratio (16.87 versus 17.93 for its peer), which could suggest that the company might be presenting a more attractive value proposition right now than its peer. We will, of course, want to investigate this a bit further, which will be done over the remainder of this article.

In addition to having a lower price-to-earnings ratio than its closest peer, ONE Gas also has a very attractive ratio compared to the S&P 500 Index (SP500). As just stated, ONE Gas has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 16.87 right now. This is better than the current forward and trailing price-to-earnings ratios of the S&P 500 Index, even following the recent market carnage. As we can see here, the index currently has a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 29.11 to 19.76 depending on whether we use the estimates for the second quarter of 2024 or the estimates for the fourth quarter of 2026:

The current trailing price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 Index is 25.75:

The trailing price-to-earnings ratio for ONE Gas is 18.41, so that is also well below that of the index. Value investors might appreciate this as the current ratios mean that an investor is paying less for each dollar of ONE Gas’s earnings than for that of the average stock in the market.

In addition to a low price-to-earnings ratio, ONE Gas also has a reasonably attractive dividend yield. The common stock yields 3.73% today, which is well above the 1.42% of the S&P 500 Index (SP500). However, ONE Gas currently has a yield that is only in line with that of its peers:

Company Name

3.71%

2.40%

NiSource Inc.

3.01%

Northwest Natural Holding Company

4.89%

3.83%

We see a very similar yield to Southwest Gas Holdings, but given that the price is more attractive, it might make this company a better investment than its peers. Let us take a closer look at ONE Gas now.

Recent Performance

My last article on ONE Gas was published on June 20, 2023, so almost two years have passed since our last discussion. Unfortunately, the stock has had a rather disappointing performance since that time. The stock price is down 8.25%:

While the utility sector, iShares U.S. Utilities ETF (IDU) in general was pretty weak in 2023, it rebounded last year and overall delivered a very competitive performance to the S&P 500 Index since that date. ONE Gas did not participate in the utility rebound to any great extent, though. This could partially explain the apparent attractiveness of its valuation, but there could also be a reason for the disappointing performance.

About ONE Gas

As stated in the introduction, ONE Gas is a regulated natural gas utility that serves approximately 2.3 million customers in Texas, Oklahoma, and Kansas. As is the case with Southwest Gas Holdings, ONE Gas is a pure-play natural gas utility and does not have any electric generation or distribution capacity. For some investors, this might be a deal-breaker since electricity is expected to experience much greater demand growth going forward. After all, some drivers of electricity consumption growth, such as artificial intelligence data centers and an increase in the popularity of electric cars, will not be relevant here. With that said, though, there is no reason to expect the demand for natural gas to go away since it is much cheaper and more efficient to heat a building with natural gas than it is with electricity. The U.S. Energy Information Administration makes this very clear in its 2024 Winter Fuels Outlook. Depending on where an individual lives, space heating can be a significant portion of their budget, so the lower cost and higher efficiency of natural gas compared to electric heating will likely keep the demand for natural gas utility services at stable levels.

In the case of ONE Gas, there could also be some demand growth going forward. It will likely not be news to anyone that Texas has been one of the more rapidly growing states in recent years. According to World Population Review, the population of Texas is growing at a 1.8% rate. This is expected to fall off slightly as the decade progresses, but projections still assign a 1.7% growth rate to the Texan population as late as 2029:

This actually gives Texas the second-highest growth rate in the United States, second only to Florida. It appears that a sizable proportion of Texas’s population growth is occurring within ONE Gas’s service territory. The two largest cities (by population) that ONE Gas serves in Texas are Austin and El Paso. Austin is in Travis County, which has had a cumulative growth rate of 4.1% since 2020. El Paso is in El Paso County, which has had a much lower cumulative growth rate of 0.99% since 2020.

El Paso County is, unfortunately, the slowest growing of Texas’s most populous counties. It is expected that this will continue to be the case going forward, as no significant growth drivers have been identified for the region. El Paso County is technically close to the Permian Basin, but so far, the jobs in the energy industry have not attracted a large number of people to this particular county. As I stated in a recent article, I am somewhat doubtful that the industry will experience a massive production surge and thus a sufficient increase in available jobs to attract more people to this county. Thus, there does not appear to be any reason to expect that this segment of ONE Gas’s service territory will experience more rapid growth going forward.

Austin, on the other hand, maybe a different story. The growth rate of this city remains one of the highest in the country as industries such as information technology continue to move to the city and create jobs there. This naturally attracts individuals who can do those jobs, and these individuals demand heat in their homes. Restaurants and similar businesses also open up in the area to service these newly relocated workers, and these businesses will also need natural gas utility service to cook food, heat their buildings, and other things. As the natural gas utility provider in this area, ONE Gas is positioned to experience growth as the growing population causes new residential customers and businesses to demand utility hookups and provide incremental revenue for the company through the payment of their monthly bills.

ONE Gas appears to recognize the opportunity that exists from Texas’s growing population. The company has budgeted $311 million for capital investments in the state this year that will be used to expand, modernize, and upgrade the utility infrastructure that it possesses:

We can see that this is a very substantial 65.42% increase over the capital spending that the company devoted to its Texas operations back in 2021. This is a greater increase in capital spending than was the case for either of the other two states in which ONE Gas operates over the period:

State

Capital Spending 2021

Planning Capital Spending 2025

% Change

Texas

$188 million

$311 million

+65.42%

Oklahoma

$215 million

$288 million

+33.95%

Kansas

$139 million

$151 million

+8.63%

Perhaps surprisingly, the company’s planned capital spending in both Kansas and Oklahoma is expected to be lower in 2025 than in 2024:

This is a rare occurrence for any utility company. In many cases, a utility company has to increase its capital spending from year to year just to overcome the impact of depreciation, let alone generate any real growth. It is not that the populations of these states are shrinking, either. Oklahoma’s population growth rate is 0.77% annually, and Kansas’s is a bit lower at 0.64% annually. The company’s service territories in both Oklahoma and Kansas include some of the best cities in the country to move to, at least according to Forbes. Oklahoma City, Oklahoma, Wichita, Kansas, and Tulsa, Oklahoma are all on the Forbes list and are all within ONE Gas’s service territory. However, it is worth noting that Wichita, Kansas did suffer from a population decline over the 2016 to 2020 period. The population of Wichita still continues to decline, with World Population Review stating that it had a negative 0.16% growth rate in the most recent year. This could be a reason for the drop-off in capital investment in those two states as the demographics are not as strong as Texas, and the company does not have unlimited capital. However, the company has not stated this directly. Its guidance does state that 24% of its 2025 capital spending is intended to allow its infrastructure to service additional customers, though, so it would be a reasonable guess that it is directing resources to locations where population growth is strongest.

Long-Term Projections

Population growth will be a major driver of ONE Gas’s growth going forward, which even the company admits. However, it will not be the company’s only source of growth. The company’s rate base growth will account for a substantial proportion of its growth going forward. ONE Gas has budgeted $750 million for capital spending on infrastructure in 2025. Only $180 million of this is intended to expand the infrastructure sufficiently to accommodate the company’s customer growth. The remainder will be spent on things such as infrastructure upgrades, system maintenance, and similar projects. This does not necessarily mean that the money is wasted, though, since this spending on things not directly related to customer acquisition will still increase the company’s rate base to approximately $5.80 billion from $5.52 billion at the end of 2024.

ONE Gas has submitted rate increase requests to the regulators overseeing its operations in the three states that would allow it to boost the prices that it charges customers. This is a direct result of the increase in the company’s rate base due to capital spending. As of right now, it has three pending requests (two in Texas, one in Oklahoma) that have yet to be approved:

Historically, it seems unlikely that these requests will be rejected. This will therefore provide an avenue for growth for the company that is not dependent upon adding new customers.

ONE Gas intends to continue to generate growth via this method going forward. Over the 2025 to 2029 period, ONE Gas’s most recent capital plan calls for it to invest approximately $4 billion in its infrastructure. About $1 billion of this will be used to allow it to service additional customers within its service territory as the population grows. Thus, the growth capital is expected to be about 25% of total capital spending, which is in line with the figure that we calculated earlier for 2025 alone. Overall, this should allow the company to grow its rate base at a 7% to 9% rate through 2029. Assuming that regulators allow the company to continue to earn a return on equity similar to the figures used in the most recent rate filings, the company’s net income should grow at a similar 7% to 9% rate over the 2025 to 2029 period.

However, it is worth noting that ONE Gas will need to issue some equity to finance these planned capital expenditures. If it were to finance them entirely with debt, its financial structure would become too debt-heavy and risky. The company’s guidance states that it will require about $1.5 billion in external capital injections to finance this plan as discussed. Management has stated that about $600 million of this will come from new equity issuance, which will dilute the existing shareholders somewhat. It will also increase the company’s debt ratios relative to both its equity and cash flow, but it should still remain within tolerable levels. The fact that the shareholders will be diluted does mean that they will not receive the full benefit of the projected 7% to 9% net income growth through 2029. Once this is considered, the earnings per share growth drops to about 4% to 6% over the period, assuming the share price remains around $70 to $75 per share. That is, of course, not guaranteed. The company has already entered into forward sale agreements to sell 403,000 new shares at $75, though, so that is a start (although it does only cover about 5% of the $600 million that it needs to raise).

It is worth noting that the calculated 4% to 6% growth rate is lower than Southwest Gas Holdings is likely to deliver over the same period. As such, the lower price-to-earnings ratio for ONE Gas might make a certain amount of sense.

Financial Considerations

ONE Gas generally has a pretty reasonable amount of leverage relative to its peers. At the time of my previous article on this company, it had a 1.11 net debt-to-equity ratio, which was lower than any other natural gas utility except for Atmos Energy. Here is how it compares today:

(all figures as of December 30, 2024)

As shown, ONE Gas continues to have a stronger balance sheet than any of its peers except for Atmos Energy. This is something that risk-averse investors will likely appreciate given the risks involved with the use of debt. It is also very nice to see when we consider that the company’s capital spending plan seems likely to increase the ratio going forward. As it is much less leveraged than some of its peers, the company appears to have some leeway to boost its debt without taking on too much risk.

Valuation

According to Zacks Investment Research, ONE Gas will grow its earnings per share at a 5.86% rate over the next three to five years. That is at the high end of the projections made earlier, but still reasonable. This growth rate figure gives the company a price-to-earnings growth ratio of 2.88 at the current share price.

Here is how ONE Gas compares with its peers:

On the surface, there are certainly some reasons to believe that ONE Gas is very attractively priced. It has a low forward price-to-earnings ratio relative to the market, it serves some of the most rapidly growing cities in the United States, and its market capitalization and enterprise values are lower than the comparably sized Southwest Gas. However, the company looks a bit less attractive when we consider the fact that its impending shareholder dilution offsets some projected net income growth. When we take that into account, the company starts to look rather expensive.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant System agrees that ONE Gas is not as attractively priced as it might appear on the surface. The system has assigned a valuation grade of “C” to the stock:

This is not as good as some of the company’s peers:
