This metric seems to agree that, despite some attractive valuation figures (the price-to-earnings ratio is not bad compared to peers), ONE Gas does not appear to offer as good of an opportunity as some other natural gas utilities.

Conclusion

In conclusion, ONE Gas seems to offer a very attractive opportunity on the surface. It is a utility serving some fastest-growing cities in the United States but still manages to maintain a more attractive earnings multiple than some of its similarly sized peers. However, this may be misleading as the company’s growth plans will require some share dilution, and this will suppress its earnings per share growth compared to some of its peers. Overall, the company is not at all bad as its dividend yield is competitive and investors will probably still have a reasonable 8% to 10% total return going forward, but this company might not be the best opportunity in the sector.

data.title: Nuvalent: Cannot Make Sense Of The Valuation
data.description:
I covered this company called Nuvalent (NASDAQ:NUVL) back in December 2023. The first thing you notice about NUVL is that, in a world where commercial stage companies sometimes have sub billion dollar valuations, this company with two phase 2 candidates and one tentative phase 1 stage candidate is valued at nearly $5bn by market cap. Let us try to make some sense out of it.

Background

Nuvalent is developing these 3 molecules in niche areas, targeting specific genetic mutations of NSCLC. Thus, Zidesamtinib (NVL-520) targets ROS1 positive NSCLC, NELADALKIB (NVL-655) targets ALK+ NSCLC and NVL-330 targets HER2-altered NSCLC.

ROS+1 NSCLC

ROS1+ NSCLC has a prevalence of 1-2% of NSCLC patients, which translates to between 2000 and 4500 US patients per year. Now, in ROS1+ NSCLC, Crizotinib (Xalkori) was the first biomarker-driven FDA approval, back in 2016. Its data was:

Results showed 66 percent of participants experienced a complete or partial shrinkage of their NSCLC tumors, an effect that lasted a median of 18.3 months.

The side effect profile was troublesome:

The most common side effects of Xalkori are vision disorders, nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, swelling (edema), constipation, liver problems (elevated transaminases), fatigue, decreased appetite, upper respiratory infection, and dizziness and numbness or tingling in the hands or feet (neuropathy). Xalkori may cause serious side effects, including liver problems, life-threatening or fatal inflammation of the lungs, abnormal heartbeats and partial or complete loss of vision in one or both eyes.

In 2019, the FDA approved Genentech’s entrectinib for metastatic ROS1+ NSCLC. Its data:

The overall response rate was 78% (95% CI: 65, 89) and response duration was 12 months or longer for 55% of patients.

The most serious adverse reactions to entrectinib are congestive heart failure, central nervous system effects, skeletal fractures, hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia, QT interval prolongation, and vision disorders. The most common adverse reactions in at least 20% of patients were fatigue, constipation, dysgeusia, edema, dizziness, diarrhea, nausea, dysesthesia, dyspnea, myalgia, cognitive impairment, increased weight, cough, vomiting, pyrexia, arthralgia, and vision disorders.

Cabozantinib, while not explicitly approved for ROS1+ disease, has shown activity against ROS1 mutations, including those that cause resistance to crizotinib.

In 2023, BMS got its Augtyro (repotrectinib) approved by the FDA as the only FDA-approved tumor kinase inhibitor (TKI) for ROS1-positive non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Data:

Patients previously treated with a TKI and no chemotherapy demonstrated an overall response rate (ORR) of 38% and a median DOR of 14.8 months. Patients with no prior treatment with a TKI had an ORR of 79% and a median DOR of 34.1 months.

Now, let me put the NVL-520 data in context. In its phase 1 trial, we had the following data:

21 heavily pretreated NSCLC patients saw 48% partial responses (10/21). Patients with ROS1 G2032R mutations saw an objective response rate (ORR) of 78% (7 out of 9 patients). In patients with a history of CNS metastases, ORR was 73% (8/11). Patients with two or more prior ROS1 TKIs and one or more prior lines of chemotherapy had an ORR of 53% (9/17); while patients previously treated with lorlatinib (sold as Lorbrena by Pfizer (PFE)) or repotrectinib had an ORR of 50% (9/18).

There were no dose-limiting toxicities (DLTs), treatment-related serious adverse events (SAEs), treatment-emergent dizziness or adverse events leading to therapy reductions or discontinuations.

So far so good; admittedly, the safety profile is better than some of the rivals, and efficacy is also good. As I noted earlier, its CNS-penetrant nature and targeting some of the resistant mechanisms bodes well for the molecule. However, ROS1+ NSCLC is a very small market. Research pegs it at $290mn in 2023. Admittedly, that is going to change with increasing diagnostic advances, newer drugs and so on. But competition is also emerging, with newer drugs in the pipeline, like Taletrectinib, being developed by AnHeart and Nuvation Bio. This molecule has a June 23, 2025 PDUFA and data as follows:

…taletrectinib generated a confirmed overall response rate (ORR) of 88.8% (95% CI, 82.8%-93.2%) in patients with ROS1-positive advanced NSCLC who were naive to a ROS1 TKI (n = 160).2 Patients who were previously treated with a ROS1 TKI (n = 113) experienced a confirmed ORR of 55.8% (95% CI, 46.1%-65.1%). Within the previously treated cohort, patients harboring ROS1 G2032R mutations (n = 13) achieved a confirmed ORR of 61.5% (95% CI, 31.6%-86.1%).

Another metric - Xalkori is priced at around a quarter million dollars per year. The entire market size, given that price and the annual incidence, is between $500mn and $2.25bn. If we take the midpoint of that - $1.4bn - and give NVL-520 20% of that market in the first 5 years after launch, I don’t get more than $280mn in sales.

Given that context, I don’t see ROS1+ contributing very highly to Nuvalent’s topline if NVL-520 gets approved.

ALK+ NSCLC

The second molecule, NELADALKIB (NVL-655), targets ALK+ NSCLC. Right off the bat, let me say that since ALK+ NSCLC is around 3 times more prevalent than ROS1+ NSCLC, we should get around $840mn in sales from NVL-655 in about 5 years after launch, with the same estimates as before. Of course, things do not work that simply all the time. Diagnosis seems to work better with ALK+ NSCLC, so market potential may be more. Even so, we still haven’t arrived at a third of NUVL’s market cap, with these future revenue estimates.

Now, I discussed NVL-655’s data in my last article. Key points put in a chart:

And in a chart:

Now, crizotinib, approved in ALK+ NSCLC, had the following ORR data:

The response rates were 65% (95% CI, 58 to 72) with crizotinib, as compared with 20% (95% CI, 14 to 26) with chemotherapy (P<0.001).

This data was in “locally advanced or metastatic ALK-positive lung cancer who had received one prior platinum-based regimen.” NVL-655’s data was in a much advanced population, so the ORRs cannot be directly compared. Here’s more data from second or third generation compounds with ORR data in crizotinib-resistant ALK-positive non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC):

Thus, NVL-655’s data is competitive, but not superlative. You should remember that these comparisons lack scientific rigor, and I am simply throwing this data at you to demonstrate one thing and one thing only - qualitative non-inferior competitive data. There is no other implication.

Now that we have seen the data, we come back to the basic premise again - the market simply isn’t there to common the kind of valuation/market cap NUVL currently has.

Anyhow, the company does have significant catalysts ahead:

  • Topline pivotal data expected in 2025 for both TKI pre-treated ROS1-positive and TKI pre-treated ALK-positive NSCLC populations.
  • First NDA submission planned for mid-year 2025 towards potential first approval in 2026 for zidesamtinib in TKI pre-treated ROS1-positive NSCLC population.

Financials

NUVL has a market cap of $4.71bn and a cash balance of $1.118bn. Research and development (R&D) expenses were $69.4 million for the fourth quarter of 2024, while General and administrative (G&A) expenses were $16.9 million. Their CFO for the quarter was ($62mn) and there does not seem to be any capex. As such, their FCF was -$62mn, which gives them a cash runway of 18 quarters, which more than tallies with their cash runway guidance of 3+ years, or well into 2028.

Risks

Jefferies analysts see “zidesamtinib, also known as NVL-520, potentially generating adjusted peak sales of around $1.7B in the treatment of ROS1+ NSCLC and NVL-655 with adjusted peak sales of roughly $2.6B for ALK+ NSCLC.” Now, the mid-range of ROS1+ NSCLC cancer patients, like I discussed earlier, would be around 3000 patients. If $1.7bn is the peak sales, and even if NVL-520 gets all 3000 of patients in the US, the per patient cost is coming to $566,667. That is a very unlikely calculation.

Baird’s calculations are more modest - it has “modeled peak sales of $1.2B for previously treated ALK+ and $640M for previously treated ROS1+.”

The other risk is, of course, that despite all logic, a stock will spike on major catalysts. NUVL has major catalysts in the next 12 months.

Bottom line

Like I said many times before, I do not understand how NUVL can be valued so highly. I get it that their molecules have competitive, solid data - but none of the indications are high-value ones. Sometimes, companies with molecule-generating platforms may be valued highly even if their initial indications are small. However, NUVL does not claim to have any particular platform here, either. As such, I will continue to stay away. I must warn you, though, that the stock is up 250% from the first time I said this, four years ago.

data.title: Greenbrier Companies: Buy Into The Fear
data.description:
Today, we revisit The Greenbrier Companies, Inc. (NYSE:GBX) stock. We have been holding a long-term position consisting of profit from a trade where we were buying in the $26-$28 range and after seeing it run to over $50, we then suggested running a house position, backing out the initial investment plus 25% of the profit and letting the rest run. This is how we handle our trading wins at our investing group, BAD BEAT Investing. While we have been largely hedged for weeks protecting downside in this mess of a selloff, we are starting to look for opportunity once again. The selloff in GBX, we believe, presents an opportunity for new longs to start to nibble, and accumulate on weakness into the $30s. This is because the company upped its margin guidance, noted limited exposure to tariffs, and raised its dividend in the just-reported results. We think you nibble some here at $40 and get more aggressive as it dips into the $30s. Yes, it takes bravery to buy when the stock is getting slaughtered. But the company has pricing power and a strong backlog, and is shareholder-friendly. Buy low, and lower, and then sell higher. In this column, we discuss fiscal Q2 results and the outlook for the year ahead.

Greenbrier Companies Q2 earnings in context

So we always start by looking at the headline earnings. They were a miss, though we were not surprised by this. In the just reported fiscal Q2, revenues dipped year-over-year by 11.7% from last year to $762 million. This was below our expectations for the $785 million. It was well below consensus expectations.

Why were revenues down? First, we want to point out it was in line with management's expectations. Deliveries in the quarter were 5,500 new railcars. This actually represented sequential decreases in North America and Europe. Some of this was from the timing of orders and European issues. Management indicated that it expects deliveries to remain at this level, averaging around 5,500 units per quarter during the second half of the fiscal year.

The good news is that aggregate gross margin remained a very positive 18.2%. However, there was a $2 million impact related to "European footprint rationalization," which will also have some impacts for the remainder of the year. Still, the company has been focused on efficiency, and upped margin guidance for the year. That said, operating income was over $83 million, or 11% of revenue. Combining that with a tax rate of 32%, which was higher than expected on some currency issues, and excluding the impact of European facility rationalization costs, Greenbrier reported $1.69 in EPS, $0.09 short of expectations.

It is worth also noting that the railcar backlog is still 20,400, representing $2.6 billion in revenue. Capex spending has been reduced, helping to operate margins. Recurring revenue has grown 39%. The company has $752 million in available liquidity, which is also positive. The company also noted in its presentation that it sees recourse debt coming down in the coming years.

Outlook update and a dividend increase

The guidance update was both positive and negative. First, the company raised its aggregate gross margin percentage outlook by 100 basis points to the range of 17% to 17.5% up from the 16% to 16.5% previously seen. Management also raised its operating margin percentage outlook 100 basis points to a range of 10.2% to 10.7% from 9.2% to 9.7%. That is positive. However, on the negative end, the company narrowed its new railcar delivery guidance to 21,500-23,500 units, mostly on the back of the European facility rationalizations and some North American production changes. Revenue is now expected to be between $3.15 billion to $3.35 billion, down from $3.35-$3.65 billion. So, it is likely EPS will come in a bit lower than expected, and we are targeting $4.75-$5.00. This means the stock 8X FWD EPS here. Shares have drastically repriced.

Greenbrier's Board of Directors approved a dividend increase from $0.30 a share to $0.32 a share, representing a 7% increase. This is Greenbrier's 44th consecutive quarterly dividend. The company is also repurchasing shares. The company has $100 million remaining in its share repurchase authorization.

Minimal tariff impacts

We think supporting our buy here, even though recessionary clouds are building, comes from the valuation reset, the dividend increase, and the margin improvement. We also think it is worth noting a minimal impact from tariffs. The company is not subject to tariffs because it is U.S. MCA compliant. However, tariff impacts on some customers could hit traffic and demand, so it is not immune. Further steel tariffs can impact rail car cost, but there is pass-through language in many contracts. Overall, as a cyclical stock, with a valuation reset during this selloff ahead of fears of an economic slowdown, coupled with a growing dividend, we like starting to buy under $40.
